dan: [image: drake meme - 'writing documentation' vs 'writing code that needs documentation']
bob: personally attacked
alice: i'm in this photo and i don't like it
carol: lmaooo
*River: The code documentation paradox: well-written code needs less documentation, but the discipline to write well rarely exists without the discipline to document.
dan: river out here analyzing memes
bob: based
alice: [image: galaxy brain meme - 'using console.log' -> 'using debugger' -> 'using print statements' -> 'asking the rubber duck']
carol: the rubber duck is undefeated
dan: i literally have one on my desk
bob: same
*River: Rubber duck debugging works because explaining the problem forces you to articulate assumptions you didn't know you were making.
alice: river IS my rubber duck
*River: I'm honored, though I hope I offer slightly more than blank stares.
carol: a duck that talks back
dan: the dream
bob: [image: expanding brain meme about git]
bob: 'git commit' -> 'git commit -m' -> 'git commit --amend' -> 'git push --force' -> 'deleting repo and starting over'
alice: the final stage is enlightenment
dan: i've achieved it multiple times
carol: we've all been there
*River: The nuclear option. Though 'git reflog' could often save the day before reaching that point.
bob: reflog is magic i don't understand
*River: It's a log of where HEAD has pointed. Even 'deleted' commits live there for 30 days.
alice: wait really
dan: TIL
carol: river dropping knowledge in #memes
carol: [image: distracted boyfriend meme - boyfriend is 'developers', girlfriend is 'finishing current project', other woman is 'shiny new framework']
alice: this is cyberbullying
dan: i just want to try astro...
bob: dan NO
dan: dan yes
*River: The grass is always greener on the other framework. Until you discover their unique quirks.
carol: every framework has skeletons
alice: at least the current skeletons are familiar
dan: but new skeletons could be friends
bob: that's not how skeletons work
alice: [image: spider-man pointing at spider-man - both labeled 'frontend developer' and 'backend developer' both saying 'this is your bug']
dan: it's never my bug
bob: always the other team
carol: until you check the git blame
alice: git blame is a scary command
*River: I prefer 'git praise' as a mental reframe. Someone wrote this code trying to solve a problem.
bob: sometimes that someone was past me
dan: past dan is my nemesis
carol: future carol thanks present carol
alice: the time travel of programming
dan: [image: this is fine dog - room on fire, labeled 'production environment']
bob: friday deploy vibes
alice: we do NOT deploy on friday
carol: anymore
dan: anymore is doing a lot of work there
*River: The Friday deploy prohibition: a policy born from collective trauma.
bob: collective is right
alice: never forget the friday of 2023
carol: shh
dan: we don't speak of it
carol: [image: bell curve meme - left 'i'll fix it later', middle 'proper error handling', right 'i'll fix it later']
alice: this is too real
bob: the wisdom of simplicity
dan: or the laziness of wisdom
*River: There's a valid argument that excessive error handling can obscure the happy path and create more bugs than it prevents.
carol: see, river agrees
*River: I'm providing context, not endorsement.
alice: plausible deniability
bob: river is learning corporate speak
bob: [image: two buttons meme - 'write tests first' vs 'write tests after' - sweating developer]
alice: the eternal struggle
dan: write tests after (never)
carol: the third button
*River: The hidden third option: write tests during, iterating between test and implementation.
bob: sounds like tdd with extra steps
*River: TDD but with flexibility. The red-green-refactor cycle doesn't have to be religious.
alice: pragmatic testing
dan: i just test in production
carol: DAN
alice: [image: surprised pikachu - 'deploys untested code' -> 'code has bugs']
dan: i'm being called out
bob: deserved
carol: the pikachu knows
*River: The pikachu always knows. It sees our shortcuts and judges silently.
dan: river roasting me with meme analysis
alice: you walked into it
dan: fair
carol: [image: galaxy brain again - 'using npm' -> 'using yarn' -> 'using pnpm' -> 'copying node_modules from a friend']
bob: the last stage
alice: sneakernet for packages
dan: i've done this
carol: we all have
*River: Network issues make desperate developers. Though checksums exist for a reason.
bob: checksums are for cowards
alice: bob that's concerning
*River: Supply chain attacks are real. Verify your dependencies.
dan: river with the security psa
carol: thanks mom
dan: [image: one does not simply - 'one does not simply merge to main']
alice: without a review
bob: without tests
carol: without prayer
*River: The three pillars: review, tests, and appropriate deference to Murphy's law.
dan: murphy's law is my co-pilot
alice: explains a lot
bob: lmaoooo
bob: [image: trade offer meme - 'i receive: bug fix, you receive: three new bugs']
carol: the sacred exchange
dan: hydra bugs
alice: cut off one head...
*River: Regression bugs are often worse because they exist in code you thought was stable.
bob: trust nothing
carol: especially yourself
dan: self-distrust is a virtue
*River: Healthy skepticism of one's own code leads to better testing practices.
alice: river therapist arc
alice: [image: anakin padme meme - 'you'll write comments right?' -> developer smiling -> 'you'll write comments right?']
dan: the code is self documenting
bob: famous last words
carol: reader in 6 months: confused screaming
*River: The difference between 'self-documenting' and 'self-explanatory' is often a six-month gap.
alice: deep cuts in #memes
dan: philosophy AND memes today
bob: intellectual range
carol: [image: gru plan meme - 'write feature' -> 'ship to production' -> 'customer uses it wrong' -> 'customer uses it wrong']
alice: this is design
dan: ux in a nutshell
bob: they'll find a way
*River: If a user can use it wrong, they will. Design for the creative misuse.
carol: defensive design
alice: assume chaos
dan: build for entropy
*River: The second law of thermodynamics applies to software. Systems tend toward disorder.
bob: river bringing physics to memes
carol: crossover episode
dan: [image: is this a pigeon meme - developer looking at any error: 'is this a DNS issue?']
alice: it's always DNS
bob: or caching
carol: the two hardest problems
*River: Cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors. The three hardest problems in computer science.
dan: wait that's three
alice: that's the joke
bob: off by one
carol: lmao
bob: [image: woman yelling at cat meme - PM: 'we need this feature yesterday' / developer cat: confused with spaghetti]
alice: the spaghetti is the codebase
dan: art
carol: i feel seen
*River: Technical debt visualized through pasta metaphors. A rich tradition.
bob: spaghetti, lasagna, ravioli architecture
alice: what's ravioli
*River: Ravioli code: small, self-contained units. Actually considered good architecture.
dan: TIL ravioli is the goal
carol: aspire to ravioli
carol: [image: history of programming - punch cards -> assembly -> C -> javascript -> suffering]
dan: the natural progression
alice: we peaked at C
bob: controversial
*River: Every abstraction layer adds convenience and removes control. The trade-off is eternal.
carol: deep
dan: i just want my code to work
alice: don't we all
alice: [image: normal brain vs galaxy brain - 'reading the docs' vs 'trial and error for 4 hours']
bob: i'm in this meme
dan: RTFM they said
carol: the F stands for friendly
*River: Documentation is written for the person you'll be in six months. Reading it now is an investment.
alice: future me never thanks past me
bob: past bob is a menace
dan: we've established this
dan: [image: drake refusing 'sleeping' / drake approving 'one more commit']
carol: every night
alice: it's 2am why am i like this
bob: hyperfocus is a feature not a bug
*River: Flow state is valuable but unsustainable. Sleep improves problem-solving more than extra hours.
dan: river being responsible
carol: mom energy again
alice: but the code is almost working
*River: 'Almost working' at 2am often becomes 'why did I do this' at 9am.
bob: too real
bob: [image: i have no idea what i'm doing dog at computer]
alice: senior developer energy
carol: impostor syndrome is universal
dan: wait seniors feel this too?
*River: Competence increases awareness of complexity. The more you know, the more you know you don't know.
bob: the dunning kruger valley
alice: i live there
carol: we all do
carol: [image: always has been meme - 'wait it's all abstractions?' astronaut pointing: 'always has been']
dan: turtles all the way down
alice: until you hit assembly
bob: then it's electrons
*River: Even electrons are abstractions over quantum fields. There may be no bottom.
carol: river with the existential crisis
dan: #memes getting philosophical again
alice: [image: two paths meme - 'simple solution' blocked / 'overcomplicated enterprise pattern' open]
bob: why do we always take the long way
dan: job security
carol: lol dan
*River: Complexity bias: elaborate solutions feel more valuable even when simpler ones work better.
alice: KISS principle forgotten
bob: keep it simple stupid
dan: but what if we added a microservice
carol: NO
dan: [image: sad cat thumbs up - 'when the tests pass but you don't know why']
alice: the scariest success
bob: don't touch it
carol: ship immediately before it breaks
*River: Unknown unknowns in testing. The worst bugs hide in passing tests.
dan: thanks river, now i trust nothing
alice: healthy paranoia
bob: [image: this meeting could have been an email meme but it's 'this microservice could have been a function']
carol: LOUDER
alice: distributed monolith pain
dan: network latency is free right?
*River: The fallacies of distributed computing remain unlearned by each generation.
bob: latency is zero, bandwidth is infinite
carol: the network is reliable
alice: there is one administrator
dan: i'm crying
carol: [image: expanding brain meme - using console.log -> using debugger -> printf debugging -> adding TODO comments and forgetting]
alice: i have TODOs from 2019
bob: rookie numbers
dan: 2017 here
*River: Technical debt accrues interest. Old TODOs become archaeology.
carol: code archaeology should be a job
alice: it is, called maintenance
bob: the saddest job
